{{< include latex-macros/macros.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

::: notes
Fitting a proportional hazards model means estimating two pieces:
the coefficients $\vb = (\beta_1, \ldots, \beta_p)$ that govern the covariate effects,
and the baseline hazard $\haz_0(t)$.
We estimate them in two stages:

1. estimate $\vb$ by maximizing a *partial likelihood* in which the unknown baseline $\haz_0(t)$ cancels out,
   so the covariate effects can be fit without committing to a shape for the baseline;
2. then, with $\hvb$ in hand, estimate the baseline cumulative hazard $\cuhaz_0(t)$ by the Breslow estimator.

Throughout, assume for simplicity that there are no tied event times;
let $t_1 < t_2 < \cdots < t_K$ be the distinct event times for the whole data set,
let $R(t_i)$ be the risk set at time $t_i$,
and let $(i)$ denote the subject who fails at $t_i$ (unique under the no-ties assumption).
Recall the model:
:::

$$
\ba
\diffloghaz(\vx) &\eqdef \beta_1x_{1}+\cdots+\beta_p x_{p}
\\
\hazfactor(\vx) &= \expf{\diffloghaz(\vx)}
\\
\haz(t|\vx) &= \blue{\haz_0(t)}\,\red{\expf{\diffloghaz(\vx)}}
\\
&= \red{\hazfactor(\vx)}\,\blue{\haz_0(t)}
\ea
$$

{{< slidebreak >}}

::: notes
**Stage 1: estimating the coefficients $\vb$.**
The key idea is to condition on *when* the failures happen and ask only *which* at-risk subject failed at each event time.
Over a short interval of width $\Delta t$,
the probability that an at-risk subject $j$ fails at $t_i$ is approximately its hazard times the interval width,
$\haz(t_i \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t = \blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}$.
So, conditional on exactly one failure at $t_i$ among the risk set $R(t_i)$,
the probability that the subject who failed is subject $(i)$ is that subject's share of the total —
and the shared baseline factor $\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}$ cancels:
:::

$$
\ba
\Pr(\text{subject } (i) \text{ fails} \mid \text{1 failure at } t_i,\ R(t_i))
&= \frac{\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}}{\sum_{k \in R(t_i)}\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_k)}}
\\
&= \frac{\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}}{\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \red{\hazfactor(\vx_k)}}
\ea
$$

::: notes
Because the baseline factor cancels, this conditional probability depends only on $\vb$.
Multiplying these probabilities across the $K$ event times gives the **partial likelihood**:
:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_def-ph-partial-lik.qmd >}}

::: notes
To prove that this partial likelihood is free of the baseline hazard,
we first assemble a chain of general discretized-likelihood building blocks —
used only by this proof, so we develop them here rather than in the
[Intro to Survival Analysis](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd) chapter.
One piece carries over from that chapter:
the [interval failure probability](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-interval-fail-prob) $q_j(\tau)$
(introduced ahead of that chapter's Kaplan-Meier section, since the
Kaplan-Meier derivation also relies on it).
From it we build its first-order approximation,
the censored-data likelihood,
the exact discretized survival factor,
the per-interval likelihood factor,
and the likelihood regrouped by interval:
:::

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_def-first-order-interval-fail-prob.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_lem-surv-discretized.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_lem-censored-lik.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_def-interval-lik-factor.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_lem-lik-by-interval.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

::: notes
Under the proportional-hazards model, the first-order interval failure probability
splits into a shared baseline factor and a subject-specific hazard ratio:
:::

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_def-ph-first-order-interval-fail-prob.qmd >}}

::: notes
We then build up to the partial likelihood through a chain of proportional-hazards lemmas:
the single-subject failure probability and the two factors of each event
(*whether* a failure occurs and *which* subject it is),
and finally the assembly that cancels the baseline hazard.
:::

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_lem-ph-single-subj-fail.qmd >}}

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_def-ph-risk-set-score.qmd >}}

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_lem-ph-whether-failure.qmd >}}

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_lem-ph-event-chain-rule.qmd >}}

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_lem-ph-which-subject.qmd >}}

::: notes
With the lemmas in hand, the proof assembles them and shows the baseline cancels —
confirming that the product is free of $\haz_0$:
:::

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_proof-ph-partial-lik.qmd >}}

::: notes
We maximize this partial likelihood numerically with respect to $\vb$
(which determines $\diffloghaz(\vx) = \tp{\vx}\vb$ and $\hazfactor(\vx) = \expf{\diffloghaz(\vx)}$).
When there are tied event times some adjustments are needed, but the likelihood is similar.
Crucially, the baseline hazard never enters, so we obtain $\hvb$ without estimating $\haz_0$.
:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

::: notes
**Stage 2: estimating the baseline hazard.**
Once we have the coefficient estimates $\hvb = (\hat\beta_1, \ldots, \hat\beta_p)$ —
and hence $\hat\diffloghaz(\vx)$ and $\hat\hazfactor(\vx)$ —
the baseline cumulative hazard is estimated by the Breslow estimator:
:::

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_def-breslow-baseline-cuhaz-est.qmd >}}

::: notes
This estimator reduces to the Nelson-Aalen estimator when there are no covariates;
several other estimators have also been proposed.
To derive it as a profile likelihood, substitute the proportional-hazards
decomposition into the full censored-data likelihood
(@lem-censored-lik):
:::

$$
\Lik\sb{\vb,\,\haz_0(\cdot)}
= \prod_{j = 1}^{n}
    \sb{\blue{\haz_0(\tilde{T}_j)}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}}^{\delta_j}\,
    \expf{-\blue{\cuhaz_0(\tilde{T}_j)}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}},
$$ {#eq-ph-breslow-full-lik}

::: notes
where $\delta_j$ is the event indicator and $\tilde{T}_j$ the observed time for subject $j$
— the same quantities denoted $D_j$ and $Y_j$ in the censored-data likelihood lemma,
so $\tilde{T}_j = Y_j = \min(T_j, C_j)$ and $\delta_j = D_j$ —
using $\haz(t \mid \vx) = \blue{\haz_0(t)}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx)}$ (@thm-ph-haz-decomp)
and $\surv(t \mid \vx) = \expf{-\blue{\cuhaz_0(t)}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx)}}$
(by @thm-ph-cuhaz, using $\surv(t) = \expf{-\cuhaz(t)}$ from the
[survival/cumulative hazard relationship](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#cor-surv-int-haz)).
Fix $\vb$ and maximize over $\haz_0(\cdot)$.
We build up to the Breslow estimator's profile-likelihood justification through a chain of lemmas:
the point-mass form of the maximizing hazard, the resulting profile likelihood,
the maximizer of each point mass, and the assembly that recovers the partial likelihood.
:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_lem-ph-breslow-point-mass.qmd >}}

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_lem-ph-breslow-profile-lik.qmd >}}

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_lem-ph-breslow-mle.qmd >}}

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_lem-ph-breslow-closes-loop.qmd >}}

::: notes
With the lemmas in hand, the proof assembles them into the Breslow estimator
and shows the profile likelihood recovers the partial likelihood:
:::

{{< include _subfiles/proportional-hazards-models/_proof-breslow-baseline-cuhaz-est.qmd >}}
